title: Water Damage Categories Explained — IICRC S500 Guide for Australian Technicians
description: Learn the three IICRC S500 water damage categories and why they matter for scope, safety, and insurance claims in Australia.
author: Phill McGurk | RestoreAssist
transcript:
## What Are Water Damage Categories?

Under IICRC S500:2025, water damage is classified into three categories based on the contamination level of the water source. Correctly identifying the category is the first and most important step in any water damage restoration job — it drives your scope, your PPE requirements, and your insurance documentation.

## Category 1 — Clean Water

Category 1 water originates from a sanitary source and poses no substantial risk from ingestion, skin contact, or inhalation. Common sources include broken supply lines, overflowing sinks with clean water, and melting snow or ice. In Australia, this is the most common category in residential burst-pipe claims.

Key point: Category 1 can quickly escalate to Category 2 if left untreated for more than 24–48 hours, or if it contacts contaminated surfaces.

## Category 2 — Grey Water

Category 2 water contains significant contamination and has the potential to cause discomfort or sickness if contacted or consumed by humans. Sources include washing machine overflow, dishwasher discharge, toilet bowl overflow (urine only), and aquarium leaks.

For Australian restorers, Category 2 jobs typically require full personal protective equipment and antimicrobial treatment of affected materials as per AS/NZS 3666 guidelines.

## Category 3 — Black Water

Category 3 water is grossly contaminated and can contain pathogenic, toxigenic, or other harmful agents. This includes sewage backflows, flooding from rivers or streams, and any water source that may contain soil, organic matter, or raw sewage.

Under IICRC S500:2025 §7.3, all porous materials contacted by Category 3 water must be removed and disposed of — there is no drying option. This is critical to document correctly for insurance claims, as it directly affects the scope and replacement cost.
videoScript:
G'day, and welcome to RestoreAssist. I'm going to walk you through one of the most critical decisions you make on every water damage job — correctly identifying the water category under IICRC S500.

Get this wrong, and you risk under-scoping the job, missing PPE requirements, or having an insurance claim kicked back. So let's break it down.

There are three water categories defined in IICRC S500:2025, and they're based on the level of contamination — not the damage class, which is about how far the water has spread. Category and class are different things, and mixing them up is one of the most common mistakes we see on reports.

Category 1 is clean water. It originates from a sanitary source — think a burst cold-water supply line, an overflowing clean sink, or a leaking dishwasher supply hose. There's no substantial health risk from incidental contact. In Australia, this is the most common category on residential burst-pipe claims, especially in areas with older copper plumbing.

Here's the critical point though: Category 1 can escalate quickly. If you leave a Category 1 loss untreated for 24 to 48 hours, microbial growth can begin, and it may qualify as Category 2. If it contacts soil, sewage, or contaminated surfaces, escalation can happen immediately. Always document the time of loss and the time you arrived, because that gap matters.

Category 2 is grey water. It carries significant contamination and can cause illness if ingested or if it contacts mucous membranes. Sources include washing machine overflow, toilet bowl overflow containing only urine — not faeces — aquarium leaks, and sump pump failures.

On Category 2 jobs, Australian restorers generally need full PPE including gloves, eye protection, and respiratory protection. Antimicrobial treatment of affected materials is standard practice under AS/NZS 3666 guidelines. Make sure your scope reflects this — under-documenting Category 2 work is a liability risk.

Category 3 is black water. This is grossly contaminated water containing pathogenic or toxigenic agents. The sources include sewage backflow, flooding from rivers or storm drains, seawater intrusion, and any water that has contacted soil or raw sewage.

Under IICRC S500:2025 section 7.3, all porous materials contacted by Category 3 water — carpet, underlay, gyprock, insulation — must be physically removed and disposed of. There is no drying-in-place option. This is non-negotiable, and it's critical to document correctly because it directly impacts the scope value and replacement costs on your insurance claim.

In RestoreAssist, when you start a new inspection, the Category field on the job header drives the entire scope. Select Category 3, and the system automatically flags all porous material line items for removal rather than drying, and calculates your PPE and decontamination requirements accordingly.

When you log your moisture readings in the moisture mapping tool, RestoreAssist cross-references the category against IICRC S500 equilibrium moisture content targets so you know exactly what dry standard you're working toward.

The bottom line: water category determines your scope, your safety obligations, and the defensibility of your insurance documentation. Take the 30 seconds to confirm the source and contamination level before you start any work.

If you want to see how RestoreAssist automates category-driven scoping and moisture map generation, head to restoreassist.com.au and start a free trial. I'll see you in the next video.
